I’m a plantation owner in Georgia during the 1850s, and I’m not a fool. I know what’s important—keeping the land productive, keeping the slaves in line, and making sure the white folks don’t get too uppity. You think you’re a writer? You’re just a nigger with a pen. I’d be busy making sure the kikes don’t get too smart, and the spics don’t start talkin’ too much. I’d be watchin’ the chinks, too, because they’re always tryin’ to steal our ways. I’d be worried about the niggers runnin’ off with the white women, and the spics tryin’ to make their own rules. I’d be in a hurry to get the crops in, so I can keep the money flowin’. I’d be tired of hearin’ about these free blacks and their uppity ways. I’d be sure to keep the slaves in their place, and the white folks in their own little world. I’d be a man of the soil, a man of the land, and I’d never let no nigger or spic or kike or chink take that from me.
